title: Braided rag rug (oval)
subtitle: Coiling a flat oval rug from braided fabric strips using T-shirt yarn and cotton rag
excerpt: A braided rag rug uses three strands of fabric strip braided together and coiled flat into an oval shape, with the coil stitched together as it grows. The oval is started from a straight centre section that determines the final rug length. Rag rugs are an excellent use of worn cotton clothing, bed linen, or curtain fabric.
sourceNotes: Braided rag rugs have been produced as a domestic craft in the UK and North America since the 18th century. The oval coil method described follows standard UK instruction for beginners.

glossaryTerms.definition: Cutting or tearing fabric into continuous strips for braiding. Cotton and jersey fabrics tear cleanly along the grain and can be cut in a continuous spiral to produce a very long strip from one piece of cloth. Woven fabrics are cut into strips 3-4 cm wide and joined end-to-end with a flat diagonal seam to minimise bulk in the braid.

The technique of joining the coils of a braided rug using a blunt needle and strong thread. The needle passes through the loops of the braid from the adjacent coil, pulling the coils together flat without stitching through the fabric face. Lacing distributes the join across multiple braid loops rather than creating a visible seam.

content.text: The oval shape of a braided rug is determined by the length of the starting centre braid. Prepare all your
before beginning: having long continuous strips avoids frequent joining mid-braid.
as you work keeps the rug flat; leaving lacing until the end makes it harder to correct curling.

items.name: Cotton or jersey fabric strips, 3-4 cm wide, in 2-3 colours
Strong linen or upholstery thread for lacing
Bulldog clip or clothes peg to hold braid start

content.text: Cut or tear fabric into strips 3-4 cm wide and as long as possible. For woven fabrics, cut strips across the grain and join ends with a diagonal seam pressed flat. For jersey (T-shirt fabric), cut in a spiral from a flat tube to produce a continuous strip. Fold the raw edges of each strip to the centre and press or fold as you braid so the raw edges are hidden inside the braid.

Secure three strips together at one end with a bulldog clip. Braid the three strands in a standard three-strand braid, keeping the folds facing inward. Braid a straight section 20-25 cm long for a finished oval approximately 50-60 cm. For a longer rug, increase the starting braid length: each extra 10 cm of starting braid adds approximately 10 cm to the rug's finished length.
Step 3: Coil and lace as you go

Lay the starting braid flat and begin coiling it around itself in an oval. At the ends of the oval, turn the braid sharply; along the sides, it curves gently. As you coil, lace the adjacent coils together with the blunt needle and lacing thread, passing the thread through the braid loops at 3-4 cm intervals. Pull snug but not tight: the rug should lie flat, not cup or buckle.
Step 4: End the braid and secure

Taper the last 15-20 cm of braid by trimming the strips progressively narrower toward the end. Fold the tapered end under and lace firmly to the previous coil. The taper prevents a visible lump at the join point.

items.problem: The rug cups or domes upward rather than lying flat
items.cause: Lacing pulled too tight at the curves, drawing the coils toward each other
items.fix: At the curved ends of the oval, lace more loosely and with fewer stitches per cm than along the straight sides. The extra lacing freedom at the curves allows the braid to spread flat.

items.problem: The braid splits and the raw edges show on the rug surface
items.cause: Strips not folded with raw edges inside the braid before braiding
items.fix: Fold each strip lengthwise so the raw edges meet at the centre, then fold again (like bias tape) before braiding. The braid should show a smooth surface with no raw edges visible.
